regions.name: Mosel River Valley -2016
regions.excerpt: Where we fell in love with riesling...
regions.review:
Rachel had a work trip to Amsterdam. I decided to tag along. And instead of doing what most people do — stay in the city, hit the tourist spots — we asked ourselves a simple question: what's within driving distance? One Google search later, I saw a photo of a breathtaking valley with a castle sitting on top of a hill, a river winding through tiny storybook towns, and vineyards climbing so high they didn't look real. I clicked the link and I couldn't stop reading. The place was called Cochem. We had to go.
There was only one wrinkle. The Moselle is Riesling country — and back in 2016, when I thought of Riesling I thought of one thing: sweet wine. The kind that sits in the back of the wine shop. Not exactly what gets you excited about a spontaneous road trip. But something told me to trust it. So we rented a car in Amsterdam, drove four hours through some of the most beautiful scenery I've ever seen, and checked into a little family-run B&B right on the river. A hundred euros a night. Top floor room. Everything we needed.
Oh — and Rachel had just found out she was expecting our second child. So I held down the fort and tasted for the both of us. She was a good sport about it.
What happened next changed how I think about wine.
We found a small winery doing tastings and sat down with three Rieslings — dry, off-dry, and sweet. And I sat there genuinely stunned. The dry Riesling was crisp, refreshing, sophisticated. Nothing like what I expected. The off-dry had just enough fruit to make it interesting without tipping into sweetness. And the sweet? Even that was elegant — more like a Sauterne than the sugary stuff I'd written off years ago. I never thought I would look at a Riesling the way I look at a Sauvignon Blanc or a Sancerre. That afternoon I did. One grape. So many magical things.
We spent five days driving town to town through the valley. Every stop had different Rieslings with different personalities. Dry for early evening on the terrace. A touch sweeter with dinner. And here's the thing — I felt like a champion every morning. No heaviness, no fog. That's the high acidity in Riesling — it's as refreshing the next day as it is in the glass.
The vineyards themselves are something you have to see to believe. The Moselle has the steepest vineyard slopes of any wine region in Europe. So steep that harvesting by hand is nearly impossible — they use these horizontal rail carts to move along the hillside because no one can safely stand on the incline. When you see it from the valley floor, it's breathtaking.
And the best part? This region is accessible and affordable in a way that very few wine destinations are. You don't need a big budget to do this right.
Travel Tips:
Plan for at least five days — the towns are small and the pace is slow, and that's exactly the point. Start in Cochem and work your way through the valley. The biggest city is Trier, the oldest city in all of Germany, and well worth a stop. The journey ends beautifully in Koblenz, where the Moselle meets the Rhine. Keep in mind that English isn't widely spoken in the smaller towns, so come with patience and an open heart. The food is very German — hearty, unfussy, and honest. Embrace it. This region doesn't put on a show for tourists, and that's what makes it so special.
The Kazmi Take: Go. Don't overthink it, don't wait for the perfect moment. Rent a car, book a little B&B on the river, and drive from town to town. The Moselle will meet you with open arms.
